Application To File Motion Under Seal; Memorandum Of Points & Authorities
Off Calendar Jul-09-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF QOINS TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s Application To File Motion Under Seal.

Judgment Creditor Qoins Technologies, Inc.'s application to file motion under seal is denied. In order for a California trial court to issue a sealing order, the court must expressly find, among other things, that "A substantial probability exists that the overriding interest will be prejudiced if the record is not sealed;" "The proposed sealing is narrowly tailored;" and "No less restrictive means exist to achieve the overriding interest. (CRC 2.550(d). Even assuming that Qoins has identified an overriding interest that supports sealing, Qoins' application, based solely on an attorney declaration, provides only conclusions as to the quoted matters that the court must find.(CRC 2.551 (b)(1) (The sealing "application must be accompanied by a memorandum and a declaration containing facts sufficient to justify the sealing.")). The pertinent sentence in the declaration is "Giving notice of Qoins' discovery would only permit Bond to carry out this fraudulent scheme further." Why? By whom? How so? How do you know? These and possibly other key questions are left unanswered.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling.

Judge: Harold E. Kahn; Clerk; W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/HEK)

DEMURRER to COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-25-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: DEFENDANT BOND FINANCIAL TECHNOLOGIES, INC., DEMURRER to COMPLAINT.

No appearance was made by any Party, Counsel, or Interested Person.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant's demurrer to complaint is overruled. The court grants defendant's unopposed request for judicial notice. Plaintiff sufficiently alleges a "wrongful act" to support the interference claims. (Korea Supply Co. v. Lockheed Martin Corp. (2003) 29 Cal.4th 1134, 1158-1159.) "The unauthorized transfer of property constitutes a conversion." (Welco Elecs., Inc. v. Mora (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 202, 209; see also ESG Cap. Partners, LP v. Stratos, 828 F.3d 1023, 1038 (9th Cir. 2016) ["an unauthorized transfer of funds is sufficient to constitute disposal of a plaintiff's funds in a manner inconsistent with the plaintiff's rights."].) Here, the complaint alleges that defendant "inexplicably and without any authority" initiated the return of plaintiff's funds to SouthState. (Complaint, par. 25; see also pars. 5, 26.) Defendant cites no authority holding that its unilateral unauthorized decision to send plaintiff's money to a third party-even if it was plaintiff's bank/former bank-cannot be deemed wrongful. A factual issue remains whether the transfer damaged plaintiff. For example, perhaps plaintiff could no longer gain access to the funds once defendant gave them to SouthState. Defendant fails to show that the economic loss rule bars the interference claims. "[T]he economic loss rule prevents the law of contract and the law of tort from dissolving one into the other." (Robinson Helicopter Co. v. Dana Corp. (2004) 34 Cal.4th 979, 988 (quotations omitted).) When a defendant's contractual breach economically injures a plaintiff, the plaintiff ordinarily must recover through a contract claim, not a tort claim. (Erlich v. Menezes (1999) 21 Cal.4th 543, 552.) However, a breach of contract can also be actionable as a tort when the conduct violates a separate duty arising from tort law. (Erlich, 21 Cal.4th at 552-554.) Conduct that breaches a contract can therefore provide the basis for an intentional interference claim as long as it is independently tortious. "[C]ontract actions are created to enforce the intentions of the parties to the agreement, [while] tort law is primarily designed to vindicate social policy." (Foley v. Interactive Data Corp. (1988) 47 Cal.3d 654, 683 (quotations omitted).) Here, defendant's intentional and unauthorized transfer of plaintiff's funds sufficiently alleges a breach independent of the parties' Program Agreement. In fact, while defendant argues that its conduct was within the ambit of the agreement, no party provides a copy of the agreement so its scope is unclear. The demurrer to the restitution cause of action is overruled. Defendant correctly notes that a restitution claim fails where the parties have an express contract that covers the same subject matter. (Hedging Concepts, Inc. v. First All. Mortg. Co. (1996) 41 Cal.App.4th 1410, 1420.) But in this case, as noted, the court does not know the terms of the Program Agreement and whether it applies to the pleaded facts.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not reported. (302/RBU)
